---
title: "Aula Conectada"
subtitle: "Investment case · Bolivia"
author: "Hernando Grueso, PhD <br> UNICEF"
date: "June 2026"
lang: en
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, slides_theme.scss]
    logo: ""
    footer: "Aula Conectada · Investment Case"
    slide-number: c/t
    transition: fade
    incremental: false
    smaller: false
    embed-resources: true
    fig-align: center
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r setup}
suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(dplyr); library(tidyr); library(readr); library(here)
  library(glue); library(knitr); library(stringr)
  library(purrr); library(tibble)
})

# Load saved results
funnel  <- read_csv(here("output", "tables", "t8_target_funnel.csv"),
                    show_col_types = FALSE)
coefs   <- read_csv(here("output", "models", "main_lpm_coefs.csv"),
                    show_col_types = FALSE)
inter   <- read_csv(here("output", "models", "intersectional_coefs.csv"),
                    show_col_types = FALSE)

fmt_n   <- function(x) format(round(x), big.mark = ",", scientific = FALSE)
fmt_pct <- function(x, d = 1) sprintf(paste0("%.", d, "f%%"), 100 * x)
fmt_pp  <- function(x) ifelse(is.na(x), "", sprintf("%+.1f", x))

# Significance stars helper
stars <- function(p) ifelse(is.na(p), "",
                     ifelse(p < 0.01, "***",
                     ifelse(p < 0.05, "**",
                     ifelse(p < 0.10, "*", ""))))

# English label mappings
term_en <- c(
  female      = "Girl (1=yes)",
  rural       = "Rural",
  poor_d      = "Poor (INE)",
  indigenous  = "Indigenous",
  disab_any   = "Disability (WG3+)"
)
outcome_en <- c(
  attending      = "Attending school",
  enrolled       = "Enrolled",
  grade_delay    = "Age-for-grade delay",
  dropout        = "Dropout",
  neet           = "NEET",
  hh_internet    = "HH internet",
  hh_computer    = "HH computer",
  hh_any_device  = "Any HH device"
)

n_girls_total    <- funnel$n_pop[funnel$segment == "All girls 10–19 (weighted)"]
n_girls_priority <- funnel$n_pop[grepl("priority", funnel$segment)]
share_priority   <- n_girls_priority / n_girls_total

# Pre-built tables -----------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Main coefficients per outcome (pp with stars)
main_pp <- coefs |>
  filter(term %in% names(term_en)) |>
  mutate(
    pred = factor(term_en[term], levels = unname(term_en)),
    cell = paste0(fmt_pp(estimate_pp), " pp", stars(p.value))
  ) |>
  select(outcome, pred, cell) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = outcome, values_from = cell)

# Journal-style table builder: two rows per predictor (coef, then SE in parens)
build_journal_table <- function(outcome_vars, outcome_labels) {
  long <- coefs |>
    filter(outcome %in% outcome_vars, term %in% names(term_en)) |>
    mutate(
      predictor = as.character(term_en[term]),
      outcome   = as.character(outcome_labels[outcome]),
      coef_cell = sprintf("%.3f%s", estimate, stars(p.value)),
      se_cell   = sprintf("(%.3f)", std.error)
    ) |>
    select(predictor, outcome, coef_cell, se_cell)

  pred_order <- unname(term_en)
  out_order  <- unname(outcome_labels)

  rows <- purrr::map_dfr(pred_order, function(p) {
    sub <- long |> filter(predictor == p)
    coef_vec <- setNames(sub$coef_cell, sub$outcome)[out_order]
    se_vec   <- setNames(sub$se_cell,   sub$outcome)[out_order]
    coef_row <- as.list(c(Variable = p,  coef_vec))
    se_row   <- as.list(c(Variable = "", se_vec))
    bind_rows(tibble::as_tibble(coef_row), tibble::as_tibble(se_row))
  })
  rows
}

# Intersectional coefficients (interaction × Girl)
inter_pp <- inter |>
  mutate(
    estimate_pp = estimate * 100,
    interaction_label = case_when(
      interaction == "female_rural" ~ "Girl × Rural",
      interaction == "female_indig" ~ "Girl × Indigenous",
      interaction == "female_disab" ~ "Girl × Disability",
      interaction == "female_poor"  ~ "Girl × Poor",
      TRUE ~ interaction
    ),
    cell = paste0(fmt_pp(estimate_pp), " pp", stars(p.value))
  ) |>
  filter(outcome %in% c("attending", "enrolled", "hh_internet", "hh_any_device",
                        "dropout", "neet")) |>
  select(outcome, interaction_label, cell) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = outcome, values_from = cell) |>
  # Replace any NA cells with dashes (occurs when regression couldn't converge
  # for a given outcome × interaction, e.g. for neet with limited variation
  # in some interaction cells)
  mutate(across(-interaction_label, ~ ifelse(is.na(.) | . == "NA", "—", .)))
```

# 1 · Diagnostic — gaps in education {.section-divider background-color="#00377C"}

## Target population — adolescent girls 10–19 in Bolivia {.smaller}

```{r funnel-table}
segment_en <- c(
  "All girls 10–19 (weighted)"                                              = "All girls 10–19",
  "Girls 10–19 — rural"                                                     = "Rural girls 10–19",
  "Girls 10–19 — Indigenous"                                                = "Indigenous girls 10–19",
  "Girls 10–19 — poor (INE)"                                                = "Girls in poverty (INE) 10–19",
  "Girls 10–19 — disability (WG 3+)"                                        = "Girls with disability (WG 3+) 10–19",
  "Girls 10–19 — no HH internet"                                            = "Girls without HH internet 10–19",
  "Girls 10–19 — priority (rural OR Indigenous OR poor OR disability)"      = "Priority girls (rural ∨ Indigenous ∨ poor ∨ disability)"
)
funnel |>
  filter(segment != "All adolescents 10–19 (weighted)") |>
  mutate(
    Segment           = recode(segment, !!!segment_en),
    `Weighted N`      = fmt_n(n_pop),
    `% of girls 10–19`= fmt_pct(share_of_all_girls)
  ) |>
  select(Segment, `Weighted N`, `% of girls 10–19`) |>
  kable(align = "lrr")
```

::: footer
Source: INE Bolivia, EH 2024 · weighted estimates
:::

## Gender gaps in education

![](../output/figures/f1a_edu_overall_by_sex_en.png){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

## Gender gaps by adolescent stage

![](../output/figures/f1a_age_edu_by_sex_ageband_en.png){fig-align="center" width="90%"}

::: footer
NEET is only informative for 15-19 (in 10-14 most are in school by construction).
:::

## Education outcomes by area of residence

![](../output/figures/f1b_edu_by_sex_rural_en.png){fig-align="center" width="90%"}

## Education outcomes by indigenous status

![](../output/figures/f1c_edu_by_sex_indig_en.png){fig-align="center" width="90%"}

## Education outcomes by poverty status

![](../output/figures/f1d_edu_by_sex_poverty_en.png){fig-align="center" width="90%"}

## Education outcomes by disability

![](../output/figures/f5_disability_gap_en.png){fig-align="center" width="90%"}

## Digital access is a territorial and socioeconomic gap {.smaller}

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
EH 2024 data are at the household level and therefore do not allow direct measurement of girls' and boys' individual access. At the household level, differences by sex of the adolescent are minimal. **The relevant differences are observed between rural and urban households, indigenous and non-indigenous, and by poverty status.**
:::

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Dimensions of the digital divide**

- Connectivity and infrastructure in rural areas
- Device access in indigenous households
- Economic affordability of internet and devices
- Digital skills and meaningful use

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Implication for the programme**

- Determining whether a gender digital divide exists at the individual level would require data on use, skills, and autonomy that EH 2024 does not capture
- At the household level, access gaps concentrate in the same subgroups that face the largest education gaps
- Aula Conectada acts on institutional access and pedagogical use, not on household access

:::
:::

::: footer
For complementary evidence on individual digital skills see AGETIC (National Survey on ICT). For primary-level learning gaps see ERCE 2019 (UNESCO/LLECE).
:::

## Digital divide — departmental variation

![](../output/figures/f6_internet_by_department_en.png){fig-align="center" width="80%"}

## Household digital access — no differences by sex

![](../output/figures/f1_digital_access_sex_age_en.png){fig-align="center" width="90%"}

## Connected households show marginally higher school attendance for girls

![](../output/figures/f4_attendance_by_internet_en.png){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

::: footer
**Note:** this is correlation, not causation. Households with internet also tend to have higher income, urban location, and parental education.
:::

# 2 · Evidence → Theory of Change {.section-divider background-color="#00377C"}

## What the international evidence tells us {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Consistent findings**

- Isolated hardware rarely improves learning <br><small>(De Melo et al., 2014; Cristia et al., 2017)</small>
- Digital tools work **with structured pedagogy** <br><small>(Gómez-Ruiz & Franco, 2023)</small>
- Teacher training is the central mechanism <br><small>(Olugbade et al., 2025; Bassi et al., 2016)</small>
- Gender-responsive content improves **STEM aspirations and persistence** <br><small>(Outes-León et al., 2020; Porter & Trzesniewski, 2020)</small>

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Parenting layer**

- Parenting interventions reduce **physical and emotional violence against girls** (IRR 0.55) <br><small>(Cluver et al., 2017 — South Africa)</small>
- Digital parenting apps improve **non-violent practices and parent-daughter communication** <br><small>(Janowski et al., 2025 — Tanzania)</small>
- Restrictive gender norms at home are a structural barrier to learning, attendance and the STEM transition

:::
:::

## Programme components — inputs and activities {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Inputs**

- Gender-responsive materials and safe access: digital content with female representation and free of gender bias
- STEM modules designed to retain and engage girls — a key gap in the STEM transition in Bolivia
- Connectivity and devices prioritised by need/vulnerability (rural, indigenous) rather than blanket delivery to schools

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Activities**

- Gender-transformative pedagogy and teacher training, with curricular integration of female role models
- Parental engagement for norm shift and linkage to STEM
- Girls' empowerment, leadership and agency — beyond mere participation
- Socio-emotional learning and life skills, based on the literature review
- Mentoring and accompaniment

:::
:::

## Programme components — outputs and outcomes {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Outputs**

- Data systems tracking sex-disaggregated participation and deeper learning outcomes
- Teachers trained, classrooms equipped, and content in use
- Girls integrated in STEM activities, with priority groups explicitly identified

**Short-term outcomes**

- Teacher practices that promote girls' active participation
- Support for STEM transition pathways
- Strengthened digital confidence and skills
- Safe and supportive learning environments

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Medium-term outcomes**

- Better attendance, progression and learning
- Higher interest and persistence in STEM
- Reduced household violence against girls, through the parenting layer
- Shifts in gender norms at home and school

**Long-term outcomes**

- Higher transition to tertiary education, particularly in STEM
- Better employability and reduction in wage gaps

:::
:::

## What we are building — one programme, three layers

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 14
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["① Digital access"]
        A1["Devices · connectivity<br/>digital content"]
    end
    subgraph B["② Girls' pedagogy"]
        B1["Teacher training · mentoring<br/>computational thinking"]
    end
    subgraph C["③ Parenting"]
        C1["Parent–daughter comms<br/>violence reduction"]
    end

    A1 --> R["Girls stay in<br/>school & learn"]
    B1 --> R
    C1 -.-> R
    R --> I["Narrower gender gaps<br/>in learning & work"]

    class A,A1 layerA
    class B,B1 layerB
    class C,C1 layerC
    class R,I outcome
    classDef layerA fill:#eaf6fc,stroke:#1CABE2,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#00377C
    classDef layerB fill:#fde9f2,stroke:#E2007A,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#a3005a
    classDef layerC fill:#f6f6f6,stroke:#bbbbbb,stroke-width:1px,color:#777777
    classDef outcome fill:#fff7e6,stroke:#FFC20E,stroke-width:2px,color:#7a5b00
```

<small>Quantitative anchor: **Evans & Yuan (2022)** girl-specific meta-analysis of 73+ RCTs. **Technovation Girls** (Chile) is the design template; the **parenting** layer (dashed) activates only when Bolivian data exists.</small>

# 3 · Projections — Aula Conectada in Bolivia {.section-divider background-color="#00377C"}

## Direct anchors — digital education and gender norms {.smaller}

Programmes with measured effects **on the educational outcomes** Aula Conectada seeks to improve:

```{r proj-table-direct}
proj_v2 <- read_csv(here("output", "projections", "anchor_projections_long.csv"),
                    show_col_types = FALSE)

proj_v2 |>
  filter(subgroup_id == "priority", usable_for_projection,
         !grepl("Porter", programme),
         programme_type %in% c("digital_education", "gender_norms")) |>
  transmute(
    Programme  = programme,
    Country    = country,
    `External effect (95% CI)` = ext_str,
    `φ_pop`    = sprintf("%.2f", phi_pop),
    `φ_geo`    = sprintf("%.2f", phi_geo),
    `φ_econ`   = sprintf("%.2f", phi_econ),
    `φ_deliv.` = sprintf("%.2f", phi_delivery),
    `n par.`   = n_params_used,
    `Adjusted effect (95% CI)` = adj_str,
    Sig.       = ifelse(sign(lower_95) == sign(upper_95), "✓", "—")
  ) |>
  kable(align = "lllrrrrrrl")
```

<small>**Reading the CI:** if zero is inside the interval, the anchor's original effect is not statistically distinguishable from zero — this reflects the source study's uncertainty, not an adjustment error. The **Sig.** column marks with ✓ the anchors that are statistically distinguishable from zero.</small>

## Indirect anchors — parenting layer (via SEM) {.smaller}

Parenting programmes that **do not act directly** on enrolment/attendance, but on the home environment (norms, violence). They enter the SEM as parameters of the parenting → home environment → educational outcomes pathway.

```{r proj-table-indirect}
proj_v2 |>
  filter(subgroup_id == "priority",
         programme_type == "parenting") |>
  transmute(
    Programme  = programme,
    Country    = country,
    `Original outcome` = "see A9 (SEM)",
    `φ_pop`    = sprintf("%.2f", phi_pop),
    `φ_geo`    = sprintf("%.2f", phi_geo),
    `φ_econ`   = sprintf("%.2f", phi_econ),
    `φ_deliv.` = sprintf("%.2f", phi_delivery),
    `Model use` = "input parameter for parenting → home environment"
  ) |>
  kable(align = "lllrrrrl")
```

<small>Cluver et al. (2017) reports effects on physical and emotional violence (IRR 0.55, not directly translatable to enrolment). Janowski et al. (2025) reports app engagement (modules completed). Effects propagate to educational outcomes via the SEM, not directly.</small>

## Key assumptions {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

**The five $\phi$ factors are claims, not estimates**

- $\phi_\text{pop} = 1$ when the source and target are identical in age and % rural
- $\phi_\text{baseline} = 1$ when Bolivia has full headroom; less when the indicator is already close to its ceiling
- $\phi_\text{geo}$ is banded by Haversine distance (1.00/0.85/0.65/0.50)
- $\phi_\text{econ}$ is capped at [0.30, 1.00] for stability
- $\phi_\text{delivery}$ reflects government implementation versus NGO/research

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Robustness**

- Multiplicative form is **conservative**: each $\phi \leq 1$, so the projection only attenuates the external effect, never amplifies
- Anchors are **only used** if their age range overlaps ≥ 25% with the target (10–19) and at least 2 demographic variables are comparable
- Sensitivity to alternative weightings (additive, distance-decay) in technical methods paper (in preparation)

:::
:::

## Synthesis — expected returns from Aula Conectada {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Access effect** <br>(priority group, ≈ 568K girls)

- **Anchor:** Evans & Yuan (2022) — girl-specific meta-analysis, 73 RCTs: median **+0.07 SD** on access
- **φ-adjusted to Bolivia:** **+0.06 SD** [0.02 – 0.12]
- **→ +2.4 pp attendance · −1.4 pp NEET** (girls 15–19)

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Translated to lifetime earnings** <br>(via Bolivia data)

- **+2.4 pp attendance → +0.10 attended school-years**
- **× 7.5%** Mincer return per year (EH 2024, **Bolivian women**) + NEET-exit earnings
- **→ ~$421** present value per girl → **BCR ≈ 2.8** (pedagogy bundle, $152)

:::
:::

<small>Individual digital RCTs (Porter 0.27, Mexico Learning Passport 0.26) sit near the meta-analysis's upper range — corroboration, not the anchor. Earnings are a **floor**: learning, agency, violence-reduction and intergenerational gains documented but not monetised.</small>

# 4 · Costing {.section-divider background-color="#00377C"}

## Unit costs — assumptions and sources {.smaller}

We construct **three scenarios (low / central / high)** from documented regional benchmarks. All values are annual per girl, in USD 2024.

```{r costing-table-en}
costing <- readr::read_csv(
  here::here("output", "projections", "bcr_costing_table.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
knitr::kable(costing, align = "lrrrl")
```

<small>**Key notes:** (1) Plan Ceibal reports total annualised cost of ~$75–100/child in implementation reports (OLPC News 2010, ACM 2013). (2) Bolivia teacher salaries 2024: ~Bs 3,206/mo primary (~$462) and Bs 4,947/mo secondary (~$716). (3) Rural permanence bonuses (Bs 659/mo) and per-rural-school bonuses (Bs 2,000) could reduce net cost in priority zones. (4) Figures exclude initial capital costs (electrical infrastructure, classrooms) — assumed pre-existing.</small>

## Programme scale — girls reached {.smaller}

Aula Conectada as investment in **adolescent girls 10–19**, with three priority-coverage scenarios:

```{r scale-table-en}
scale_tab <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Scenario,                                            ~`N girls`,         ~`% of total`,  ~Coverage,
  "Pilot (8 priority departments)",                     "~80,000",           "8%",           "Rural+indigenous+poor",
  "Intermediate scale",                                 "~280,000",          "29%",          "Broad priority subset",
  "Full scale (all priority girls)",                    "~568,000",          "58%",          "All girls with at least one vulnerability",
  "Universal (all girls 10–19)",                        "~977,000",          "100%",         "National coverage"
)
knitr::kable(scale_tab, align = "lrrl")
```

<small>**Population figures** derived from weighted EH 2024 (Table A1). The programme was designed for vulnerability-based targeting — we do not recommend universal coverage as it would dilute impact in households without digital access needs. **Central planning scenario** is full priority scale (~568,000 girls).</small>

# 5 · ROI — Cost-benefit analysis {.section-divider background-color="#00377C"}

## Monetised vs. broader returns {.smaller}

The BCR below monetises **only** the attendance → earnings channel. It is deliberately conservative and should be read as the **floor**, not the ceiling, of expected returns.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

**What the BCR monetises**

→ Attendance / retention gains (Evans & Yuan, φ-adjusted)

→ Bolivia Mincer return (7.5%/year)

→ NEET-exit earnings + discounted lifetime labour earnings (35 years)

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**What the BCR does NOT monetise** *(documented, not summed)*

→ **Gender norms and agency**

→ **Household violence reduction** (Cluver PLH: IRR 0.55)

→ **Parental communication** (Janowski ParentApp)

→ **Intergenerational effects** on daughters and sisters

:::
:::

These channels are **documented but deliberately excluded** from the BCR — they would raise the return, not lower it, so the monetised figure is a floor.

## Cost-effectiveness — full programme (with laptops)

Anchored on the **Evans & Yuan (2022)** girl-specific meta-analysis of access outcomes (73 RCTs): median **+0.07 SD**, φ-adjusted to Bolivia.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="13%"}
:::
::: {.column width="74%"}

| Outcome (girls 10–19) | Effectiveness | Cost per girl | Cost per 1 pp |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| **Attendance** | +2.4 pp [0.7 – 4.8] | $465 | **~$194** |
| **NEET (15–19)** | −1.4 pp [−0.4 to −2.9] | $465 | **~$332** |

:::
::: {.column width="13%"}
:::
:::

<small>*Full bundle: devices + connectivity + content + teacher training + pedagogy/M&E ≈ $465/girl over 4 years. Underlying access effect **+0.06 SD** φ-adjusted [0.02 – 0.12]. NEET is a back-of-envelope pass-through (~60% of the attendance gain).*</small>

## Cost-effectiveness — pedagogy only (without laptops)

Large RCTs of hardware-only programmes (**Cristia et al. 2017; Cueto et al. 2024**, Peru OLPC; de Melo 2014, Uruguay) find **near-zero effects on learning** — the gains come from the **pedagogy and software** layer, not the devices. Dropping hardware preserves the effect at a third of the cost.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="13%"}
:::
::: {.column width="74%"}

| Outcome (girls 10–19) | Effectiveness | Cost per girl | Cost per 1 pp |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| **Attendance** | +2.4 pp [0.7 – 4.8] | $152 | **~$63** |
| **NEET (15–19)** | −1.4 pp [−0.4 to −2.9] | $152 | **~$109** |

:::
::: {.column width="13%"}
:::
:::

<small>*Pedagogy bundle: content/platform + teacher training + pedagogy/M&E ≈ $152/girl over 4 years (no new device or connectivity procurement). The effect is held constant because the experimental evidence attributes the gain to pedagogy and software, with hardware-only arms near zero. **Cost-effectiveness improves ~3× per percentage point.***</small>

## Benefit value chain — girls 10–19 in Bolivia

How the attendance effect becomes a present-value benefit per girl (pedagogy bundle, $152/girl):

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="6%"}
:::
::: {.column width="88%"}

| Step | Calculation | Result |
|---|---|---:|
| **1 · Attendance effect** | Evans & Yuan (2022), φ-adjusted | **+2.4 pp** |
| **2 · School-years gained** | +2.4 pp attended over ~4 secondary years | **+0.10 yr** |
| **3 · Earnings — schooling** | +0.10 yr × 7.5%/yr Mincer (EH 2024 women), PV 35 yr | **~$352** |
| **4 · Earnings — NEET exit** | −1.4 pp NEET × avoided early-career idleness | **~$69** |
| **= Benefit vs. cost** | $421 benefit ÷ $152 cost | **BCR ≈ 2.8** |

:::
::: {.column width="6%"}
:::
:::

<small>The attendance gain converts directly into attended school-years (no test-score conversion needed). Central scenario [BCR range 0.8 – 5.6]. On the full programme with laptops ($465), BCR ≈ 0.9 — lower because it also funds hardware that the RCT evidence shows adds little. Counts **only earnings**: agency, violence reduction and intergenerational gains are documented but not added, so the true return is **expected to be higher**.</small>

## Key takeaways {.smaller}

**1 · The gap is territorial, not enrolment.** Enrolment is ~95% everywhere; the real gaps for girls are in **attendance, dropout, and digital access** — concentrated in rural, indigenous, and poor households.

**2 · A design anchored in girl-specific evidence.** Aula Conectada is anchored on the Evans & Yuan (2022) meta-analysis of 73 RCTs — which finds teacher-pedagogy and software (not hardware alone) are what work for girls — with Technovation Girls (Chile) as the design template.

**3 · Cost-effective when you fund the right layer.** The pedagogy bundle ($152/girl) reaches **~$63 per +1 pp of attendance** and a central **BCR ≈ 2.8** [0.8–5.6] through earnings alone. The full bundle with laptops costs 3× more for the same effect (BCR ≈ 0.9) — the RCT evidence puts the return in the pedagogy, not the hardware.

**4 · The monetised return is a floor.** The BCR counts only retention-driven earnings. Learning gains, agency, violence reduction, and intergenerational effects are documented but **not** added — the true return is higher.

# 6 · Methodological Appendix {.section-divider background-color="#00377C"}

## A0 · Cost-benefit analysis assumptions {.smaller}

- **Estimated costing.** Unit costs are projected from regional benchmarks (Plan Ceibal Uruguay, IDB EdTech reviews, Bolivia teacher salary scale). An official programme costing may refine the range — we recommend reviewing before scale-up decisions
- **Management and coordination costs.** Figures include administrative overhead (10-20%) but exclude in-country monitoring, inter-institutional coordination, and political/operational costs in remote areas. These could add 10-20% extra in rural scenarios
- **Benefits only via the education-labour channel.** We do not quantify benefits in agency, reproductive health, violence reduction, or domestic productivity — all potentially significant but harder to monetise defensibly
- **Mincer estimated from Bolivian data.** The return per year of schooling is estimated from EH 2024 (7.5%/year for women, 95% CI [6.6%, 8.4%]). The estimate does not incorporate the effect of schooling on the *probability* of employment — the total benefit is likely larger than projected here
- **3% discount rate** is the World Bank Education Sector standard; high sensitivity to discount rate (at 5% central BCR drops proportionally)
- **Analysis excludes positive externalities** on younger sisters, community norms, and intergenerational effects on future daughters — all documented in the literature but outside the scope of this conservative BCR

## A1 · Data and sample {.smaller}

::: {style="font-size:0.80em"}

**Source:** Household Survey 2024 (EH 2024) — INE Bolivia · Collection November–December 2024 · National coverage, urban and rural · Unit of analysis: members of households in private dwellings.

**Sample:** 12,718 households · 39,497 persons · Analytical subsample: **6,254 adolescents 10–19** (≈ 977K girls and 1.05M boys expanded) · Files: Persona, Vivienda, Equipamiento.

**Survey design:** Primary sampling units (`upm`), strata (`estrato`), expansion factors (`factor`). Estimates produced with `srvyr` (PSU `lonely.psu="adjust"`).

**Key variables:**

- **Sex** = `s01a_02` (1=male, 2=female) → girl = (sex == 2)
- **Age** = `s01a_03` (completed years)
- **Indigenous proxy** = childhood language OR spoken language in codes 1–37, 70, 71 (Bolivian indigenous languages)
- **Poverty** = INE-derived (`poor == 1`)
- **Disability** = Washington Group, severity ≥ 3 in any domain (seeing, hearing, walking, remembering)
- **HH internet** = `s06a_19` (Housing module) · **Digital equipment** (computer, smartphone, tablet) from Equipment module

**Estimation:** Weighted OLS using expansion factor `factor`; standard errors clustered at PSU level (`fixest::feols`).

:::

::: footer
Microdata: anda.ine.gob.bo/index.php/catalog/163
:::

## A2 · Structural synthesis of the ToC — girls 10–19 (SEM) {.smaller}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 13
flowchart LR
    LWB["Lives with<br/>both parents"]:::obs
    NP["Non-poor<br/>household"]:::obs
    ND["No disability<br/>in household"]:::obs
    ENR["Enrolled"]:::obs
    ATT["Attending<br/>class"]:::obs

    HE(("Home<br/>environment")):::latent
    EE(("School<br/>engagement")):::latent
    PR["Educational<br/>progression"]:::outcome

    LWB -.->|λ₁| HE
    NP  -.->|λ₂| HE
    ND  -.->|λ₃| HE
    ENR -.->|λ₄| EE
    ATT -.->|λ₅| EE

    HE ==>|β₁| EE
    EE ==>|β₂| PR
    HE ==>|β₃| PR

    classDef obs fill:#ffffff,stroke:#7A8487,stroke-width:1px,color:#374649
    classDef latent fill:#ffffff,stroke:#1CABE2,stroke-width:2px,color:#00377C
    classDef outcome fill:#ffffff,stroke:#E2007A,stroke-width:2px,color:#a3005a
```

<small>Latent constructs (blue ovals) are measured by observed EH 2024 indicators (grey boxes); the final outcome is educational progression (pink). The parenting layer is **excluded** — Bolivia lacks contemporary parenting data (last full MICS: 2008) — and will be added when pilot or survey data exist. Equations and estimated coefficients in A11.</small>

## A3 · Empirical strategy {.smaller}

::: {style="font-size:0.82em"}

**Base model (LPM with department fixed effects):**

$$Y_{ihd} = \alpha + \beta_F F_i + \beta_R R_i + \beta_I I_i + \beta_D D_i + \beta_P P_i + \gamma X_{ihd} + \delta_d + \varepsilon_{ihd}$$

where $F$ = Girl, $R$ = Rural, $I$ = Indigenous, $D$ = Disability, $P$ = Poor, $X$ = controls (age).

**Intersectional model with two-way interactions (× Girl):**

$$Y_{ihd} = \alpha + \beta_F F_i + \gamma X_{ihd} + \sum_{k \in \{R,I,D,P\}} \theta_k \, (F_i \times k_i) + \delta_d + \varepsilon_{ihd}$$

Each $\theta_k$ measures how much larger or smaller the gender gap is in that subgroup. For example, $\theta_R$ captures whether the gender gap between rural girls and boys differs from the gap between urban girls and boys.

**Intersectional model with three-way interactions (robustness):**

$$Y_{ihd} = \alpha + \text{main effects} + \text{2-way} + \theta_{F\cdot R \cdot I} \, (F_i \times R_i \times I_i) + \delta_d + \varepsilon_{ihd}$$

and analogous for $F \times R \times P$ and $F \times I \times P$. Three-way interactions capture whether the gender gap in a compound intersection (e.g. rural indigenous girls) differs from what the two-way interactions would suggest separately.

Estimation by weighted OLS using `factor` (expansion factor); standard errors clustered at PSU level (`fixest::feols`).

:::

## A4 · Full coefficients table — main model {.smaller}

```{r reg-table-all-en}
out_vars <- c("hh_internet", "enrolled", "attending", "hh_any_device", "grade_delay")
out_lbls <- c(hh_internet   = "HH internet",
              enrolled      = "Enrolled",
              attending     = "Attending",
              hh_any_device = "Any HH device",
              grade_delay   = "Age-for-grade delay")

tab <- build_journal_table(out_vars, out_lbls)

diag_per_outcome <- coefs |>
  filter(outcome %in% out_vars) |>
  group_by(outcome) |>
  summarise(N  = max(nobs, na.rm = TRUE),
            r2 = max(r2,   na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(out_label = unname(out_lbls[outcome]),
         N_chr  = format(N, big.mark = ","),
         r2_chr = sprintf("%.3f", r2))

n_vec  <- setNames(diag_per_outcome$N_chr,  diag_per_outcome$out_label)[unname(out_lbls)]
r2_vec <- setNames(diag_per_outcome$r2_chr, diag_per_outcome$out_label)[unname(out_lbls)]

footer_rows <- bind_rows(
  as.list(c(Variable = "N",  n_vec)),
  as.list(c(Variable = "R²", r2_vec)),
  as.list(c(Variable = "Dept FE", setNames(rep("Yes", length(out_lbls)), unname(out_lbls)))),
  as.list(c(Variable = "Weights", setNames(rep("Yes", length(out_lbls)), unname(out_lbls)))),
  as.list(c(Variable = "Cluster SE (PSU)", setNames(rep("Yes", length(out_lbls)), unname(out_lbls))))
)

bind_rows(tab, footer_rows) |>
  kable(align = c("l", rep("r", length(out_lbls))))
```

<small>Coefficients in decimal points (× 100 for pp). Robust SE in parentheses. `*` p<0.10, `**` p<0.05, `***` p<0.01.</small>

## A5 · Full intersectional table {.smaller}

```{r inter-table-full-en}
inter_outcomes <- c("attending", "enrolled", "hh_internet", "hh_any_device")
inter_labels   <- c(attending     = "Attending",
                    enrolled      = "Enrolled",
                    hh_internet   = "HH internet",
                    hh_any_device = "Any HH device")

inter_long <- inter |>
  filter(outcome %in% inter_outcomes) |>
  mutate(
    inter_lbl = case_when(
      interaction == "female_rural" ~ "Girl × Rural",
      interaction == "female_indig" ~ "Girl × Indigenous",
      interaction == "female_disab" ~ "Girl × Disability",
      interaction == "female_poor"  ~ "Girl × Poor"
    ),
    out_label = unname(inter_labels[outcome]),
    coef_cell = sprintf("%.3f%s", estimate, stars(p.value)),
    se_cell   = sprintf("(%.3f)",  std.error)
  )

inter_order <- c("Girl × Rural", "Girl × Indigenous", "Girl × Disability", "Girl × Poor")
out_order   <- unname(inter_labels)

inter_rows <- purrr::map_dfr(inter_order, function(p) {
  sub <- inter_long |> filter(inter_lbl == p)
  coef_vec <- setNames(sub$coef_cell, sub$out_label)[out_order]
  se_vec   <- setNames(sub$se_cell,   sub$out_label)[out_order]
  coef_row <- as.list(c(Interaction = p,  coef_vec))
  se_row   <- as.list(c(Interaction = "", se_vec))
  bind_rows(tibble::as_tibble(coef_row), tibble::as_tibble(se_row))
})

n_inter <- coefs |>
  filter(outcome %in% inter_outcomes) |>
  group_by(outcome) |>
  summarise(N = max(nobs, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(out_label = unname(inter_labels[outcome]),
         N_chr = format(N, big.mark = ","))
n_vec_inter <- setNames(n_inter$N_chr, n_inter$out_label)[out_order]

inter_footer <- as.list(c(Interaction = "N (each model)", n_vec_inter))

bind_rows(inter_rows, inter_footer) |>
  kable(align = c("l", rep("r", length(out_order))))
```

<small>Each row is the coefficient on the (Girl × subgroup) interaction in a separate regression. Coefficients in decimal points. Standard errors in parentheses. Robust SE clustered at PSU.</small>

## A6 · Heterogeneity analysis — regression forest {.smaller}

We complement the LPM with a regression forest (`grf::regression_forest`, Athey & Wager 2019) that estimates predicted outcomes conditional on the full set of demographic variables.

- **Training sample:** all adolescents 10–19 (not only girls) — lets the forest learn the gender effect endogenously
- **Forest:** `grf::regression_forest` with `honesty = TRUE` and 5,000 trees
- **Output:** variable importance and predicted subgroup gaps

The forest is **diagnostic, not causal** — there is no treatment in EH 2024 to estimate CATEs. Its value: identifying the demographic profiles where the (predicted) gap is largest, to guide programme targeting.

::: footer
Code: `code/04b_heterogeneity.R` · output: `output/figures/f_forest_var_importance_en.png`
:::

## A7 · Formal foundation of the transportability approach {.smaller}

We assume that the mismatch between the source population $S$ (the anchor) and the target $T$ (Bolivia) decomposes into $K$ observable dimensions, with multiplicative attenuation:

$$ \hat\beta_T \;\approx\; \hat\beta_S \; \cdot \; \prod_{k=1}^{K} \phi_k(S, T), \qquad \phi_k \in [0, 1] $$

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Glossary of $\phi_k$ factors:**

- $\phi_\text{pop}$ — standardised age and % rural
- $\phi_\text{baseline}$ — outcome headroom
- $\phi_\text{geo}$ — Haversine distance from La Paz
- $\phi_\text{econ}$ — GDP per capita ratio
- $\phi_\text{delivery}$ — 1.0 (government) or 0.7 (NGO/research)

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Assumptions:**

- (A1) **Sufficient observables**: the 5 dimensions capture the relevant effect modifiers
- (A2) **Separability**: effects are approximately multiplicative
- (A3) **Monotonicity**: mismatch only attenuates, never amplifies
- (A4) **SE independence**: anchor variance propagates without perturbing the $\phi_k$

:::
:::

## A8 · Bolivia-adjusted projection methodology {.smaller}

**Algorithm** for each anchor:

1. Compute $\phi_\text{pop}$ as $1 - \sqrt{\overline{d^2_\text{age}, d^2_\text{rural}}}$, where $d$ are standardised differences between source and Bolivia
2. Compute $\phi_\text{baseline} = (Y_\text{max} - Y_\text{Bol}) / (Y_\text{max} - Y_\text{anchor})$ when measurable (otherwise 1)
3. Compute $\phi_\text{geo}$ in Haversine bands from La Paz: ≤1,000 km → 1.00; 1,000–5,000 km → 0.85; 5,000–10,000 km → 0.65; >10,000 km → 0.50
4. Compute $\phi_\text{econ}$ as the Bolivia/anchor GDP per capita ratio, capped at [0.30, 1.00]
5. Compute $\phi_\text{delivery}$: 1.0 if government implementer; 0.7 if NGO/research (Vivalt 2020)
6. Multiply: $\hat\beta_T = \hat\beta_S \cdot \prod \phi_k$
7. Propagate SE: $\text{SE}(\hat\beta_T) = \text{SE}(\hat\beta_S) \cdot \prod \phi_k$
8. 95% CI: $\hat\beta_T \pm 1.96 \cdot \text{SE}(\hat\beta_T)$

**Exclusion rules** — age overlap <25% with 10–19, or <2 demographic variables comparable → anchor not projected.

**Uncertainty** — 95% CI propagated from the anchor SE. No synthetic noise on $\phi$ factors.

## A9 · Consolidated evidence — anchors in use {.smaller}

```{r evidence-table-en}
source(here::here("R", "anchors_demographics.R"))

anchors_demo |>
  mutate(
    arrow_en = case_when(
      programme_type == "digital_education" ~ "Digital education",
      programme_type == "parenting"         ~ "Parenting",
      programme_type == "gender_norms"      ~ "Gender norms",
      programme_type == "labor_market"      ~ "Labour market",
      TRUE                                  ~ programme_type
    ),
    Age = paste0(age_lo, "\u2011", age_hi),
    Effect = sprintf("%.3f (%.3f) %s", effect, se, unit),
    Implementer = ifelse(gov_implementer, "Government", "NGO/Research")
  ) |>
  transmute(
    Programme = programme |> stringr::str_trunc(42),
    Country   = country,
    Age,
    Effect,
    Implementer,
    `ToC arrow` = arrow_en
  ) |>
  kable(align = "lllllr")
```

<small>Complete list of external anchors used in the Bolivia projection (see `05_projections.R` and `R/anchors_demographics.R`). The full systematic review of 52 papers is in `data/lit_review_papers.csv`. The **ToC arrow** column indicates which arc of the Theory of Change each anchor feeds.</small>

## A10 · Robustness — three-way interactions (OLS) {.smaller}

To verify the heterogeneity detected by the regression forest, we estimate three-way interactions with OLS. If the forest correctly detects compound subgroups, the three-way coefficients should be significant for the outcomes with greatest heterogeneity.

```{r threeway-table-en}
threeway <- read_csv(here("output", "models", "threeway_coefs.csv"),
                     show_col_types = FALSE)

threeway |>
  mutate(
    term_en = case_when(
      str_detect(term, "female.*rural.*indigenous") ~ "Girl × Rural × Indigenous",
      str_detect(term, "female.*rural.*poor")       ~ "Girl × Rural × Poor",
      str_detect(term, "female.*indigenous.*poor") |
      str_detect(term, "female.*poor.*indigenous")  ~ "Girl × Indigenous × Poor",
      TRUE ~ term
    ),
    out_label = unname(outcome_en[outcome]),
    cell = sprintf("%.3f%s (%.3f)", estimate, stars(p.value), std.error)
  ) |>
  filter(outcome %in% c("attending", "enrolled", "hh_internet"),
         !is.na(out_label)) |>
  select(term_en, out_label, cell) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = out_label, values_from = cell) |>
  rename(`Three-way interaction` = term_en) |>
  kable(align = "lrrr")
```

<small>**How to read:** each cell shows the three-way interaction coefficient and its standard error in parentheses. Significance: `*` p<0.10, `**` p<0.05, `***` p<0.01. Where no asterisks appear, the coefficient is not statistically distinguishable from zero at conventional levels. **Interpretation:** in this sample, the three-way interactions are not significant, indicating that the compound effect of belonging to multiple vulnerable groups (e.g. rural indigenous girl) is not substantially larger than the sum of effects of each group separately. Gaps accumulate roughly additively. This suggests well-targeted interventions on any axis (rural, indigenous, poverty) will reach girls who fit those characteristics.</small>

## A11 · SEM — equations and calibration {.smaller}

**Approach**: we estimate the ToC arrows for which EH 2024 has observed indicators (attendance, dropout, age-for-grade delay, household composition as a HomeEnv proxy). The **parenting-layer parameters** ($\beta_3$, $\beta_5$) are calibrated as **informative Bayesian priors** from the $\phi$-adjusted anchors (Cluver, Janowski), not estimated from EH 2024. The structural diagram is in **A2**.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Pre-registered structural model**

$\text{GirlSkills} = \beta_2\text{ClassPractice} + \beta_3\text{HomeEnv}$

$\text{Attendance} = \beta_4\text{GirlSkills} + \beta_5\text{HomeEnv}$

$\text{Progression} = \beta_6\text{Attendance} + \beta_7\text{GirlSkills}$

```{r sem-table-en}
sem_path <- here::here("output", "models", "sem_coefs.csv")
if (file.exists(sem_path)) {
  sem_coefs <- readr::read_csv(sem_path, show_col_types = FALSE)
  sem_coefs |>
    dplyr::filter(type == "Structural path") |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      `β std.` = sprintf("%.3f", std.all),
      SE       = sprintf("(%.3f)", se),
      `p`      = sprintf("%.3f", pvalue)
    ) |>
    dplyr::select(Relationship = relationship, `β std.`, SE, `p`) |>
    knitr::kable(align = "lrrr")
}
```

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Parameter calibration**

| Parameter | Source |
|---|---|
| $\beta_2$ | Outes-Sanchez + Gómez-Ruiz ($\phi$-adjusted) |
| $\beta_3, \beta_5$ | ParentApp (Janowski) + Cluver — **prior** |
| $\beta_4$ | ELA (Bandiera) ($\phi$-adjusted) |
| $\beta_6$ | EH 2024 + pilot |

**Estimation** via `blavaan` (informative Bayesian priors), latent scales fixed at $\lambda_1 = 1$ per construct, latent errors independent. Prior uncertainty propagates to the final BCR.

:::
:::

## A12 · Bolivia Mincer estimation {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}

```{r mincer-table-en}
mincer_full <- readr::read_csv(
  here::here("output", "projections", "mincer_full.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
mincer_full |>
  dplyr::filter(term %in% c("aestudio", "exp_yrs", "exp_sq",
                            "female", "rural", "indigenous")) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    Coef = sprintf("%.4f%s", estimate,
                   dplyr::case_when(
                     p.value < 0.01 ~ "***",
                     p.value < 0.05 ~ "**",
                     p.value < 0.10 ~ "*",
                     TRUE           ~ ""
                   )),
    SE = sprintf("(%.4f)", std.error),
    Spec = dplyr::recode(specification,
                         "A: Pooled OLS" = "A",
                         "C: Female only" = "C")
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(Spec, Term = term, Coef, SE) |>
  knitr::kable(align = "llrr")
```

:::
::: {.column width="42%"}

**Headline (Spec C, female-only)**: each additional year of schooling raises expected log monthly labour income by **7.5% [95% CI: 6.6% – 8.4%]**.

This figure enters the BCR directly as the Mincer return.

**Sample**: 13,009 adults 25-60 with positive labour income (5,327 women · 2,488 rural). Weighted OLS, PSU-clustered SE. Model: `log(ylab) ~ years_edu + exp + exp² + female + rural + indigenous`.

**Caveats**: (i) OLS on the employed sample suffers from selection bias — the return may be over-estimated for that sub-sample. (ii) We do not capture the effect of schooling on the *probability* of employment — this under-estimates the total benefit.

:::
:::

## A13 · Education outcomes by age band — overall {.smaller}

![](../output/figures/f1a_age_edu_by_sex_ageband_en.png){fig-align="center" width="90%"}

::: footer
NEET is only informative for 15-19 (in 10-14 nearly everyone is enrolled by construction).
:::

## A14 · Education outcomes by age band — area of residence {.smaller}

![](../output/figures/f1b_age_edu_by_sex_rural_ageband_en.png){fig-align="center" width="92%"}

## A15 · Education outcomes by age band — indigenous status {.smaller}

![](../output/figures/f1c_age_edu_by_sex_indig_ageband_en.png){fig-align="center" width="92%"}

## A16 · Education outcomes by age band — poverty status {.smaller}

![](../output/figures/f1d_age_edu_by_sex_poverty_ageband_en.png){fig-align="center" width="92%"}

## A17 · Education outcomes by age band — disability {.smaller}

![](../output/figures/f5_age_edu_by_sex_disab_ageband_en.png){fig-align="center" width="92%"}

::: footer
Disability subsample is small (~0.4%) — interpret with caution.
:::

## A18 · Limitations

- EH measures **access to devices and internet**, not actual digital skills
- Household access **does not equal meaningful use** by the adolescent
- Disability sample size is **small** — interpret with caution
- **Cross-sectional** data: temporal trajectories cannot be observed
- Need for **direct measurement at the pilot level** to validate and refine projections

# Thank you {.section-divider background-color="#00377C"}

<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1.5em;">
<p style="color: #ffffff; font-size: 1.3em; margin-bottom: 0.3em;">Hernando Grueso, PhD</p>
<a href="mailto:hgrueso@unicef.org" style="color: #FFC20E; font-size: 1.2em; font-weight: 600; text-decoration: none;">hgrueso@unicef.org</a>
</div>
